translations.descricao: The screening for hypothyroidism aims to identify thyroid dysfunctions early to prevent cardiovascular and neurological complications. The global prevalence of subclinical hypothyroidism is approximately 4.6% in adults. In Brazil, it is estimated a prevalence of 9% in adult women. Congenital hypothyroidism affects 1 in 2,000 to 4,000 newborns.
translations.sus.indicacao: Mandatory screening for congenital hypothyroidism in all newborns. For adults, indicated in risk groups such as pregnant women, elderly, and patients with suggestive symptoms.
translations.sus.populacaoAlvo: All newborns at birth; adults with risk factors (age >60 years, family history, symptoms).
translations.sociedadesMedicas.indicacao: The American Thyroid Association (2021) recommends screening in pregnant women, postpartum, and newborns, but not routine in asymptomatic adults. The SBEM (2022) endorses screening in risk groups such as women >35 years and patients with comorbidities.
translations.epidemiologia.prevalencia: Global prevalence of overt hypothyroidism is 0.3-1%, and subclinical 4-10%. In Brazil, prevalence of 3-9% in adults, with greater impact in women. For congenital, 1:3,000 live births.
translations.epidemiologia.incidencia: Global annual incidence of 1-2 cases per 1,000 adults. In Brazil, about 1:2,500 for congenital.
translations.epidemiologia.mortalidade: Untreated hypothyroidism increases cardiovascular mortality by 20-50%. In Brazil, it contributes to 5% of deaths from associated NCDs.
